name: Ashkari
description:
Four thousand years ago, the Ashkari were an iron-age civilisation — clever, quarrelsome, and entirely unremarkable. They smelted bronze and copper on a warm, arid world of red mesas and alkaline flats, worshipped a pantheon of sky-gods, and were perhaps three centuries away from inventing the telescope. Then the sky broke. An object roughly eleven kilometres long, travelling at a significant fraction of lightspeed, struck the northern continent with enough force to vaporise a landmass the size of a small ocean. The impact winter killed an estimated sixty per cent of all life on Ashkar Prime. Cities were scoured to bedrock. Coastlines ceased to exist. Of the Ashkari population — perhaps four hundred million at the time — fewer than thirty million survived the first decade. But the object that killed their world was not an asteroid. It was a ship. Alien, incomprehensibly vast, and largely intact beneath the glass-floored crater it had carved across three thousand kilometres of continent. The Wreck, as every Ashkari has called it since before they had a written word for it, was not merely the worst catastrophe in their history. It was, eventually, their salvation — and they have never fully decided how to feel about that.

The first generations treated the Wreck as divine punishment, then as a tomb, then as a temple. Entire religions coalesced around its shattered hull, and for nearly a thousand years it was forbidden to enter the deeper sections on pain of death. But the Ashkari are, at their core, scavengers — not as an insult but as a survival strategy refined across millennia of post-apocalyptic desperation. The outer sections of the Wreck yielded materials that did not corrode, alloys that held edges forever, surfaces that generated warmth without flame. Slowly, cautiously, and often at terrible cost, the Ashkari moved deeper. They did not understand what they found. They could not read the alien script. The machinery responded to inputs they discovered by accident, trial, and the occasional explosion that levelled a district. But they learned. Generation by generation, guild by guild, secret by hoarded secret, they developed a civilisation built entirely on reverse-engineering technology that was never meant for them. They did not invent electricity — they found a conduit and worked backwards. They did not theorise spaceflight — they found an engine and spent four centuries determining which end was the front. Every fundamental technology the Ashkari possess was borrowed, decoded, and adapted from the Wreck, and this single fact has shaped their culture more profoundly than any philosophy or religion ever could. They are a species that learned to think by reading someone else's homework, and they are extraordinarily, almost unsettlingly good at it.

Ashkari society is governed by the Interpreters — a loose oligarchy of guild-masters, scholars, and intelligence brokers who control access to Wreck-derived knowledge and, by extension, to power. Information is the only currency that matters. An Ashkari guild-master's authority rests not on wealth or military strength but on what they know and whom they have taught. This creates a civilisation that is, by its very nature, built around espionage. The Ashkari did not develop intelligence-gathering as a military discipline; they developed it as a way of life. For four thousand years, their greatest minds have been engaged in the single largest act of code-breaking in galactic history — deciphering an alien civilisation's entire technological corpus from physical wreckage alone, without a Rosetta Stone, without context, without permission. When they finally reached the stars and encountered other species, they found the work almost disappointingly easy. Decoding a rival empire's encryption is trivial when your ancestors spent centuries decoding the operating principles of an alien reactor by poking it with sticks. Their diplomacy flows from the same source: warm, curious, generous with small truths, and utterly relentless in extracting large ones. An Ashkari trade delegation will learn more about your civilisation in a single state dinner than your own census bureau knows, and they will do it while being genuinely, infuriatingly charming.

The great question that haunts Ashkari civilisation is not where they came from, but where the Wreck came from. Who built it? Why was it travelling so fast? Was the crash an accident, a weapon, or something else entirely? The ship's databanks — those that survived the impact — are written in a language that four thousand years of scholarship have only partially decoded, and what little has been translated raises more questions than it answers. The Seekers, the dominant political faction, argue that the Ashkari owe their existence to the Wreck's builders and are morally obligated to find them — or to find out what happened to them. Seeker expeditions push deeper into unexplored space with every generation, following fragmentary star-charts pulled from corrupted alien databases, chasing a species that may be extinct, transcended, or simply uninterested in being found. The Founders — a growing counter-movement — argue that four millennia of living in someone else's shadow is enough. They advocate building original Ashkari technology, establishing permanent colonies, and forging an identity that does not begin and end with the Wreck. The argument is existential, and it is not theoretical. Every new alien ruin discovered, every fragment of unknown technology recovered from a derelict, every whisper of a species that might — might — match the Wreck's builders sends a tremor through Ashkari politics. They are a civilisation that bootstrapped itself to the stars on borrowed genius, and they are only now beginning to ask whether borrowed genius can ever truly become their own.
